elements.sent-2411-003: This paper investigates the enhancement of financial time series forecasting with the use of neural networks through supervised autoencoders (SAE), to improve investment strategy performance.
elements.sent-2411-004: Using the Sharpe and Information Ratios, it specifically examines the impact of noise augmentation and triple barrier labeling on risk-adjusted returns.
elements.sent-2411-005: The study focuses on Bitcoin, Litecoin, and Ethereum as the traded assets from January 1, 2016, to April 30, 2022.
elements.sent-2411-006: Findings indicate that supervised autoencoders, with balanced noise augmentation and bottleneck size, significantly boost strategy effectiveness.
elements.sent-2411-007: However, excessive noise and large bottleneck sizes can impair performance.
elements.sent-2411-009: Does data augmentation and denoising via autoencoders combined with triple barrier labeling (TBL) produce better risk-reward metrics than buy-and-hold?.
elements.sent-2411-010: Does a portfolio of SAE-MLP strategies outperform a similar portfolio of buy-and-hold cryptocurrencies in terms of risk-reward metrics?.
elements.sent-2411-011: We study three cryptocurrency pairs from January 1, 2016, to April 31, 2022, using the period from January 1, 2020, to April 31, 2022, for out-of-sample testing.
elements.sent-2411-012: Our approach employs SAE-MLP models for TBL classification using various bar intervals and a walk-forward method.
elements.sent-2411-013: Previous literature suggests that machine learning models outperform other techniques, particularly statistical approaches (e.g., ARIMA models), especially for non-stationary data (Baranochnikov and Ślepaczuk, 2022).
elements.sent-2411-014: It has been proposed that models perform better when the input value is asset price rather than return (Kijewski and Ślepaczuk, 2020).
elements.sent-2411-015: It has further been suggested that intra-day data-driven strategies should outperform inter-day data-driven strategies.
elements.sent-2411-016: For neural networks, the usage of a supervised autoencoder has been shown to improve generalization performance (Le et al., 2018), including financial time series models (Fons et al., 2020).
elements.sent-2411-017: In our study, we aim to enhance the research by applying the supervised autoencoder technique to triple barrier labeling to produce a machine-learning algorithmic trading strategy.
elements.sent-2411-018: The paper is structured as follows.
elements.sent-2411-019: Section 1 details the data and instruments used.
elements.sent-2411-021: Section 3 presents the labeling method and optimization metric.
elements.sent-2411-022: Section 4 discusses data augmentation and model architecture.
elements.sent-2411-023: Section 5 presents the results and summarizes our findings.
elements.sent-2411-024: This research utilizes three trading time series (Bitcoin, Ethereum, Litecoin) and six feature time series.
elements.sent-2411-025: The data spans from January 2016 to April 2022, with pre-2020 data used exclusively for training.
elements.sent-2411-026: Figure ~\ref{fig:pricepaths presents the logarithmic growth paths $ln\left(\frac{p_t {p_0}\right)$ for each of the traded assets.}}
elements.sent-2411-027: Each of the time series was obtained from Binance in open high low close (OHLC) format with a 1-minute bar frequency.
elements.sent-2411-028: Feature time series, sourced from FirstRateData include:.
elements.sent-2411-029: Initial Claims (ICSA): Unemployment data from FRED.
elements.sent-2411-030: WTI Crude Oil futures: Indicator of economic growth and energy demand.
elements.sent-2411-031: Henry Hub Natural Gas futures: Another energy demand indicator.
elements.sent-2411-032: Corn futures: Reflects food-related economic factors.
elements.sent-2411-034: Copper and Aluminum: Reflect industrial activity levels.
elements.sent-2411-035: These features were chosen to represent various economic sectors, mirroring factors traders might consider, spanning from unemployment and general economic activity to market uncertainty and industrial activity levels.
elements.sent-2411-036: Financial time series are notorious for their low signal-to-noise ratios, largely due to arbitrage forces in the market.
elements.sent-2411-037: Standard stationarity transformations, such as integer differentiation, often exacerbate this issue by eliminating valuable historical memory.
elements.sent-2411-038: The use of ARFIMA (Autoregressive Fractionally Integrated Moving Average) models, initially proposed by Granger and Joyeux (1980), offers an insightful perspective.
elements.sent-2411-039: These models allow for fractional differentiation, providing a more nuanced approach to maintaining data stationarity while maximizing memory retention.
elements.sent-2411-040: We consider the backshift operator $B$ applied to a time series of a feature ${X_t}$ such that $B^k X_t = X_{t-k}$.
elements.sent-2411-041: The difference between the current and last feature's value can be expressed as $\left(1-B\right)X_t$.
elements.sent-2411-042: For any real number $d$: \begin{equation}\left(1 + x\right)^d = \sum_{k=0}^\infty \binom{d}{k} x^k\end{equation} This binomial series can be expanded into a series of weights applied to feature values: \begin{equation}\omega = \left\{ 1, -d, \frac{d(d-1)}{2!}, \frac{d(d-1)(d-1)}{3!}, ..., (-1)^k \prod_{i=0}^{k-1} \frac{d-i}{k!} \right\}\end{equation} The weights can be applied to features through multiplication to construct a fractionally differentiated feature.
elements.sent-2411-043: Fractional differencing allows the generalization of differentiation to non-integer orders, capturing long-term memory while ensuring stationarity.
elements.sent-2411-044: The fixed-width window fractional differentiation (FFD) method determines the minimum coefficient $d$ that makes the fractionally differentiated series stationary (i.e., passes the Augmented Dickey-Fuller test).
elements.sent-2411-046: For a unit root, $d < 1$, while for explosive behaviors, $d > 1$.
elements.sent-2411-047: An especially intriguing scenario is $0 < d < 1$, indicating the series is "slightly non-stationary".
elements.sent-2411-048: Fractional differencing will be applied to features using a walk-forward validation methodology: Training Segment: Determine optimal $d$ for balance between memory retention and stationarity.
elements.sent-2411-049: Test Segment: Apply identified $d$ to the succeeding segment for model evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-050: Rolling Forward: Repeat the process, adapting to evolving series characteristics.
elements.sent-2411-051: The process can be summarized in the following algorithm:.
elements.algorithm-2411-001: [H] \caption{Fractional Feature Differentiation in Walk-Forward Validation} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \State Set a range of possible values for $d$ (e.g., from 0 to 1) \State Set significance level for ADF test (e.g., 1\% ) \State Initiate a dictionary associating each feature with optimal $d$. \For{each segment pair (train, test)} \For{each feature} \State Apply fractional differencing to train segment of the feature at discrete intervals \State Calculate Augmented Dickey-Fuller (ADF) test statistic and p-value for each d for a feature \State Choose lowest $d$ such that p-value < significance level. \State Save feature name and associated optimal $d$ to the dictionary \State Apply optimal $d$ differencing to both train and test set of the feature \EndFor \State Train the model on train segment, evaluate on test set \EndFor \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-052: This approach ensures features are sufficiently differenced to ensure stationarity, but the memory of the time series is still largely intact, ensuring that our model can benefit from both of the characteristics.
elements.sent-2411-053: Most modern approaches to algorithmic trading using machine learning consist of formulating the trading as a classification problem, where the predicted class describes our position (1 - long, -1 - short, 0 - no position) in the market at a given time.
elements.sent-2411-054: In this paper, we are using the triple barrier labeling method.
elements.sent-2411-055: For specified window size $\lambda$, and maximum trade length $n$ minutes, triple barrier labelling for a given time $t$ can be expressed as: \begin{equation}P_t= \begin{cases} 1, \text{if}\ \max(S_t, ..., S_{t+n}) \geq S_t \cdot (1+\lambda) -1, \text{if}\ \min(S_t, ..., S_{t+n}) \leq S_t \cdot (1-\lambda) 0, \text{otherwise} \end{cases} \label{eq:tblconditions}\end{equation} Figure ~\ref{fig:tblpicture represents the three cases visually.}
elements.formula-2411-003: \begin{equation}P_t=  1, if\ \max(S_t, ..., S_{t+n}) \geq S_t \cdot (1+\lambda) -1, if\ \min(S_t, ..., S_{t+n}) \leq S_t \cdot (1-\lambda) 0, otherwise  \label{eq:tblconditions}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-056: In the first case, the upper barrier was exceeded; therefore, we would have preferred to be long at time $t$.
elements.sent-2411-057: In the second case, none of the horizontal barriers was exceeded, so to minimize noise in results, we ideally stay out of the market in this case.
elements.sent-2411-058: In the third case, the lower barrier was exceeded, therefore our preferred position was short.
elements.sent-2411-059: This methodology also assumes in execution, each trade has take-profit and stop-loss set at their respective $S_t \cdot (1+\lambda)$ and $S_t \cdot (1-\lambda)$ levels.. .
elements.algorithm-2411-002: [H] \caption{Triple-Barrier Labeling - Simple implementation} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \State Initialize an empty series labels with the same index as prices \For{each index idx in prices} \State Set entry\_price to the price at idx \State Calculate profit\_target as entry\_price \( \times (1 + profit\_taking) \) \State Calculate stop\_loss\_target as entry\_price \( \times (1 + stop\_loss) \) \State Set time\_barrier\_idx to the minimum of idx + time\_barrier and the last index of prices \For{each price i from idx to time\_barrier\_idx} \If{prices[i] \(\geq\) profit\_target} \State Set labels[idx] to 1 \State break \ElsIf{prices[i] \(\leq\) stop\_loss\_target} \State Set labels[idx] to -1 \State break \ElsIf{i is equal to time\_barrier\_idx} \State Set labels[idx] to 0 \State break \EndIf \EndFor \EndFor \State \Return labels \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-060: It follows from Eq. ~\ref{eq:tblconditions that on correct (non-zero) prediction, our return on a given trade will always be $\lambda$ (before transactional costs).}
elements.sent-2411-061: We define such a case of taking the correct position as a "directly correct" prediction.
elements.sent-2411-062: The "directly incorrect" prediction ($Y_{true = -Y_{pred}$), on the other hand, will result in a return on the trade of $-\lambda$.}
elements.sent-2411-063: On "indirectly incorrect" prediction, for example, $(Y_{true = 0, Y_{pred =1)$ the return can be either positive or negative, depending on where the price is at $t+n$, however, will always be within $(-\lambda, \lambda)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-064: On predicting class 0 we do not open any position therefore our return on the trade will always be zero.
elements.sent-2411-065: Table 2 presents the return distribution for given predicted/true label combinations.. \begin{center Table 2.}
elements.sent-2411-066: Return on a trade given classification result.
elements.sent-2411-067: To maximize the trading strategy performance, we must introduce an error preference mechanism.
elements.sent-2411-068: After all, missing a profitable trade (type 0 error) will have a lesser effect on our portfolio than entering an incorrect trade (type 1 error).
elements.sent-2411-069: To account for that, we cannot use the accuracy metric in our models.
elements.sent-2411-070: Instead, we have to create a novel, return-maximizing metric.
elements.sent-2411-071: We define directly correct count as the number of times the model entered the correct position which resulted in the return of $\lambda$.
elements.formula-2411-004: \begin{equation}DCC = |\{ (Y_{pred}, Y_{true}) \in S \mid Y_{pred} \neq 0  and  Y_{pred} = Y_{true} \}|\end{equation}
elements.formula-2411-005: \begin{equation}DIC = |\{ (Y_{pred}, Y_{true}) \in S \mid Y_{pred} \neq 0  and  Y_{pred} \neq Y_{true} \}|\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-073: It follows from our execution assumptions that cumulative return from trades where $Y_{true \in (-1, 1)$ can be expressed as: \begin{equation}\Phi = \prod_1^{DCC} (1+\lambda) \cdot \prod_1^{DIC}(1-\lambda) = (1+\lambda)^{DCC} \cdot (1-\lambda)^{DIC}\end{equation} The above equation looks like a good contender for an optimization metric.}
elements.sent-2411-074: However, it is important to note that the above equation does not take into account the situation where we enter the trade and the vertical, time-based barrier is reached.
elements.sent-2411-075: We define the number of such trades as timed exit count (TEC).
elements.sent-2411-076: We have shown that in these cases the return on the trade will be within $(-\lambda, \lambda)$, however, we cannot assume the average trade return in these situations to be 0.
elements.sent-2411-077: We can therefore introduce a preference mechanism that discourages entering such trades, which also has a much lesser "discouragement magnitude" than for directly incorrect trades.
elements.sent-2411-078: We can do that by constructing the optimization metric as if these trades on average produce a loss, however small it may be: \begin{equation}\Phi = (1+\lambda)^{DCC} \cdot (1-\lambda)^{DIC} \cdot \left(1-\frac{\lambda}{\delta}\right)^{TEC}\end{equation} where $\delta > \lambda$.
elements.sent-2411-079: In our study, we set $\delta$ arbitrarily to 20, indicating that twenty timed exits are considered equally undesirable as one direct incorrect classification.
elements.sent-2411-080: Notably, selecting a $\delta$ value lower than $\lambda$ tends to favor trades that have historically led to timed exits.
elements.sent-2411-081: Further research is warranted to explore the potential benefits of this approach for assets characterized by consistent long-term trends, such as the S\&P 500 index.
elements.sent-2411-082: Data augmentation is a technique that has been pivotal in addressing the issues of overfitting and underrepresentation in machine learning.
elements.sent-2411-083: Originally, its use was most prominent in computer vision problems, where it significantly enhanced the performance of neural networks.
elements.sent-2411-084: Figure 9 presents exemplary data augmentation on images.
elements.sent-2411-085: In these applications, data augmentation involves making alterations to images in the training dataset to create additional training examples.
elements.sent-2411-086: These alterations can include transformations such as rotating, flipping, scaling, or altering the color balance of images.
elements.sent-2411-087: The augmented dataset thus generated presents a wider variety of scenarios for the model to learn from, which improves its ability to generalize to new, unseen images.
elements.figure-2411-003: \caption{Data augmentation example for computer vision problems.} \includegraphics[width=1.1\columnwidth]{dataaug.jpg} \caption*{ Source: Popular techniques to prevent overfitting in neural networks, datahacker.rs, Accessed May 2022}
elements.sent-2411-088: The success of data augmentation in computer vision sparked interest in its potential applicability to other areas of machine learning.
elements.sent-2411-089: In natural language processing (NLP), for example, data augmentation might involve the paraphrasing of sentences or the use of synonyms to expand the dataset.
elements.sent-2411-090: In audio processing, it could involve varying the pitch or adding background noise to sound clips.
elements.sent-2411-091: In tabular data, techniques like feature noise injection or synthetic minority over-sampling are used to enrich the datasets.
elements.sent-2411-092: Data augmentation has found a valuable place in the domain of time series analysis as well, which is the foundation for many algorithmic trading strategies.
elements.sent-2411-093: Time series data inherently carries the challenge of being sequential, where each point is temporally related to its predecessors and successors.
elements.sent-2411-094: In such a context, traditional data augmentation methods used in computer vision or NLP cannot be directly applied due to the risk of disrupting the time sequence, which is critical to the predictive nature of the data.
elements.sent-2411-095: In algorithmic trading, the time series data typically consists of historical price movements, volumes, and other financial indicators that are time-dependent.
elements.sent-2411-096: To augment this type of data, the introduction of noise to features based on a fraction of historical feature volatility is an effective technique and is the basis for data augmentation in this paper.
elements.sent-2411-097: This approach preserves the temporal structure of the data while expanding the dataset.
elements.sent-2411-098: By adding noise that is a proportion of the historical volatility, one ensures that the augmented data remains realistic and within the bounds of potential market scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-099: The noise added is typically Gaussian or drawn from a similar distribution, scaled according to the historical volatility of the feature.
elements.sent-2411-100: For example, if a particular stock has shown volatility of 2\% over a certain period, augmenting the data by adding noise with a standard deviation of 0.2\% (0.1 noise ratio) of the price feature creates new, plausible price paths for the model to learn from.
elements.sent-2411-101: This method of data augmentation helps in creating a more robust algorithmic trading strategy by forcing the model to learn not only from the historical sequence of prices but also from a range of possible price movements that could occur in real market conditions.
elements.sent-2411-102: The utility of adding noise based on historical feature volatility is twofold.
elements.sent-2411-103: Firstly, it helps in preventing overfitting by ensuring that the model does not learn to anticipate the exact historical sequence of events but rather the underlying patterns that govern market movements.
elements.sent-2411-104: Secondly, it increases the robustness of the algorithmic trading strategy by exposing the model to a wider variety of market conditions during the training phase, enhancing its ability to perform under different market scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-105: The neural network type which is examined in detail in this paper is an autoencoder, presented in Figure ~\ref{fig:sae_structure .}
elements.sent-2411-106: An autoencoder is a type of artificial neural network used to learn efficient codings of unlabeled data.
elements.sent-2411-107: The encoding is validated and refined by attempting to regenerate the input from the encoding.
elements.sent-2411-108: The autoencoder learns a representation (encoding) for a set of data, typically for dimensionality reduction, by training the network to ignore insignificant data, leading to finding the most efficient ways to compress passed data.
elements.sent-2411-109: An autoencoder consists of 3 parts: encoder, "code" (also called the bottleneck), and decoder.
elements.sent-2411-110: The encoder compresses the input and produces the code, which is the compressed, denoised data.
elements.sent-2411-111: The decoder then reconstructs the input only using the code.
elements.sent-2411-112: The metric for autoencoder performance is the similarity between reconstructed data and original input.
elements.figure-2411-004: \caption{Supervised autoencoder structure.} \includegraphics[width=1.0\columnwidth]{sae.png} \caption*{ Source: Esaú Villatoro-Tello, Shantipriya Parida, Sajit Kumar , Petr Motlicek, Applying Attention-Based Models for Detecting Cognitive Processes and Mental Health Conditions, 2021, Cognitive Computation} \label{fig:sae_structure}
elements.sent-2411-113: A supervised autoencoder (SAE) is a variation of an autoencoder that can be used for regression and classification tasks.
elements.sent-2411-114: In SAE, the encoded values are concatenated with the original input and used to train a supervised prediction model on provided labels.
elements.sent-2411-115: The performance metric of SAE is a combination of the accuracy of reconstruction of input data from code (unsupervised part) and the accuracy of predictions using concatenated original data and the code (supervised part).
elements.sent-2411-116: SAE models have shown to have improved generalization performance especially if the data is inherently noisy, which makes it a perfect candidate for a model in algorithmic trading problems.
elements.sent-2411-117: In traditional validation approaches, the dataset is split into a training set and a testing set, where the model is trained on the training set and then evaluated on the testing set.
elements.sent-2411-118: However, this approach does not accurately reflect real-world scenarios, where models need to be updated and retrained regularly to adapt to the changing patterns in the data.
elements.figure-2411-005: \caption{Walk-forward validation procedure. The training set, initially expanding, is limited to a 3-period length, therefore shifting instead of expanding since split 4.} \label{fig:wfo} \includegraphics[width=1.0\columnwidth]{wfh2.png} \caption*{ Note: Final implementation in Python 3.10 using NumPy package}
elements.sent-2411-119: Walk-forward validation, presented in Figure ~\ref{fig:wfo , is a technique that involves dividing the time series dataset into multiple overlapping windows of a constant or expanding size.}
elements.sent-2411-120: In each window, a model is trained on the first part of the window (train set) and evaluated on the second one (validation set).
elements.sent-2411-121: This process continues until the entire dataset has been used for training and testing the model.
elements.sent-2411-122: Walk-forward validation provides a more accurate estimate of the model's performance in real-world scenarios, where models need to be updated and retrained regularly.
elements.sent-2411-123: It also allows for the detection of changes in the data patterns over time, as the model is evaluated on each overlapping window.
elements.sent-2411-124: Thirdly, it ensures that the model is not overfitted to a specific portion of the dataset, as it is continuously retrained on the latest data.
elements.sent-2411-125: Finally, the constant size window fosters adaptability by requiring the model to perform well across various segments of data that reflect potential shifts in the underlying data-generating process.
elements.sent-2411-126: The walk-forward method may also be used to tune hyperparameters.
elements.sent-2411-127: A validation period follows the in-sample and is before the out-of-sample in this scenario.
elements.sent-2411-128: The walk-forward model training with the hyperparameter adjustment procedure is analogous to the process described above, with constant window size adjustments to ensure the model remains responsive to the latest data trends.
elements.sent-2411-129: In order to show how SAE-MLPs may be used in algorithmic trading, a straightforward buy-sell trading strategy is chosen based on whether the instrument price is anticipated to rise or fall over the next time period.
elements.sent-2411-130: For simplicity, we assume that the orders we place will not have an effect on the market and that they are executed instantly, at the last close price.
elements.sent-2411-131: As all three cryptocurrencies are very liquid, this assumption seems not far from the truth.
elements.sent-2411-132: If our model sends a “buy” signal, the strategy closes out a short position and takes a long position.
elements.sent-2411-133: If the long position was already taken, it leaves the position open.
elements.sent-2411-134: If the model sends a “sell” signal the algorithm takes a short position.
elements.sent-2411-135: To calculate the cumulative unrealized P\&L the following assumptions are used:.
elements.sent-2411-137: Positions can be opened in any amount, they do not have to be full units;.
elements.sent-2411-138: Transaction costs are calculated for each opening and closing of the position, which means changing position from short to long will incur double costs.
elements.sent-2411-139: Transaction cost for all assets amounts to 0.05\%.
elements.sent-2411-140: For each strategy and asset, several indicators are computed to evaluate profitability and performance.
elements.sent-2411-141: When evaluating portfolio performance, it is critical to consider not just the return but also the risk of the strategy.
elements.sent-2411-142: In the study, we utilize performance metrics from Michańków et al. (2022) and Ryś and Ślepaczuk (2018).
elements.sent-2411-143: The analysis of the trading strategy utilizing 10-minute, 20-minute, and 30-minute data for Bitcoin reveals intriguing performance characteristics when compared to a buy-and-hold approach.
elements.sent-2411-144: While the buy-and-hold strategy yielded the highest total return (406.02\%) and annualized return (61.63\%), it also exhibited the highest volatility with an annualized standard deviation of 63.31\% and a maximum drawdown of 53.30\%.
elements.sent-2411-145: In contrast, the SAE strategies demonstrated superior risk-adjusted performance, particularly evident in their information ratios (IR) and starred information ratios (IR*).
elements.sent-2411-146: Notably, the 30-minute strategy achieved the highest IR (2.03) and IR* (6.22), coupled with the lowest maximum drawdown (14.02\%) and annualized standard deviation (21.11\%), suggesting enhanced efficiency in capturing market trends while mitigating risk.
elements.sent-2411-147: The 20-minute strategy presented a balanced profile with moderate returns and risk metrics, while the 10-minute strategy, despite higher returns than its longer-interval counterparts, showed increased volatility.
elements.sent-2411-148: These findings indicate that the SAE strategies, especially the 30-minute approach, may offer more favorable risk-adjusted outcomes for Bitcoin trading compared to the buy-and-hold method, despite lower absolute returns.
elements.sent-2411-149: The analysis of Ethereum trading strategies using 10-minute, 20-minute, and 30-minute data demonstrates remarkably high returns across all approaches, including the buy-and-hold strategy, significantly outperforming the Bitcoin results previously discussed.
elements.sent-2411-150: The buy-and-hold strategy for Ethereum yielded the highest total return (1929.01\%) and annualized return (143.85\%), surpassing all tSAE strategies.
elements.sent-2411-151: However, it also exhibited the highest risk metrics with an annualized standard deviation of 82.85\% and a maximum drawdown of 62.17\%.
elements.sent-2411-152: Among the SAE strategies, the 20-minute approach showed the most favorable balance of risk and return, achieving the highest information ratio (2.64) and starred information ratio (9.51), along with a substantial total return of 1658.93\%.
elements.sent-2411-153: The 30-minute strategy, while showing lower returns, maintained comparable risk-adjusted performance metrics to the 20-minute strategy.
elements.sent-2411-154: The 10-minute strategy, despite producing higher returns than the 30-minute approach, demonstrated increased volatility and drawdown.
elements.sent-2411-155: Notably, all SAE strategies for Ethereum exhibited superior risk-adjusted performance compared to the buy-and-hold method, as evidenced by their higher information ratios and lower maximum drawdowns.
elements.sent-2411-156: This analysis suggests that for Ethereum, the 20-minute strategy may offer the optimal balance between capturing market trends and managing risk, although all strategies, including buy-and-hold, produced exceptional returns in this high-growth cryptocurrency market.
elements.sent-2411-157: The analysis of Litecoin trading strategies reveals a markedly different performance profile compared to Bitcoin and Ethereum, with SAE strategies significantly outperforming the buy-and-hold approach.
elements.sent-2411-158: Notably, the 30-minute strategy demonstrated the highest total return (382.49\%) and annualized return (59.37\%), surpassing even the 20-minute strategy and substantially outperforming the buy-and-hold method (118.92\% total return).
elements.sent-2411-159: The 30-minute approach also exhibited the most favorable risk-adjusted metrics, with the highest information ratio (2.11) and starred information ratio (4.85), coupled with the lowest annualized standard deviation (28.09\%) and maximum drawdown (25.88\%).
elements.sent-2411-160: The 20-minute strategy showed similar strengths, albeit with slightly higher risk metrics.
elements.sent-2411-161: In contrast, the 10-minute strategy and the buy-and-hold approach both underperformed significantly, with the latter showing particularly high volatility (88.61\% annualized standard deviation) and a severe maximum drawdown (75.21\%).
elements.sent-2411-162: This performance disparity underscores the potential benefits of employing SAE strategies for Litecoin trading, especially those with longer intervals.
elements.sent-2411-163: The superior performance of the 30-minute and 20-minute strategies suggests they may be more effective at capturing Litecoin's price trends while mitigating downside risk, offering a compelling alternative to both shorter-interval trading and passive holding strategies in this market.
elements.sent-2411-164: The analysis of an equally weighted portfolio comprising Bitcoin, Ethereum, and Litecoin provides valuable insights into the performance of diversified cryptocurrency strategies.
elements.sent-2411-165: Comparing the SAE approaches (10-minute, 20-minute, and 30-minute) to a buy-and-hold strategy reveals interesting dynamics in risk-adjusted returns.
elements.sent-2411-166: The 20-minute strategy achieved the highest total return (619.80\%) and annualized return (79.41\%), slightly outperforming the buy-and-hold approach (595.24\% total return, 77.57\% annualized return).
elements.sent-2411-167: However, the 30-minute strategy demonstrated superior risk-adjusted performance with the highest information ratio (2.56) and starred information ratio (7.63), coupled with the lowest annualized standard deviation (27.37\%) and maximum drawdown (23.55\%).
elements.sent-2411-168: This suggests that the 30-minute strategy most effectively balanced return generation with risk mitigation across the diversified portfolio.
elements.sent-2411-169: The buy-and-hold strategy, while competitive in terms of total return, exhibited significantly higher volatility (72.61\% annualized standard deviation) and maximum drawdown (59.46\%), underscoring the potential benefits of active management through SAE strategies.
elements.sent-2411-170: Notably, all SAE strategies outperformed the buy-and-hold approach in terms of risk-adjusted metrics, with even the 10-minute strategy showing improved risk characteristics despite lower returns.
elements.sent-2411-171: These findings indicate that for a diversified cryptocurrency portfolio, SAE strategies, particularly those with 20-minute and 30-minute intervals, may offer more favorable risk-adjusted outcomes compared to passive holding, effectively capturing market trends while mitigating portfolio-wide risks.
elements.figure-2411-012: [H] \includegraphics[width=0.70\linewidth]{pfolio.png} \caption{Equity value index for equally weighted portfolio strategies.} \label{fig:pfo_curves}
elements.figure-2411-013: [H] \includegraphics[width=0.70\linewidth]{pfolio_results.png} \caption{Performance metrics for equally weighted portfolio strategies.} \label{fig:pfo_metrics}
elements.sent-2411-172: Although the results look promising we note that Figure ~\ref{fig:corr demonstrates that the returns of each strategy are highly correlated.}
elements.sent-2411-173: We have chosen the currencies that offered the best trade-off between market cap and history length for testing using data from the last day of in-sample data.
elements.sent-2411-174: We note that the test set remained a highly bullish time for these assets, and the results will be affected by that.
elements.figure-2411-014: \includegraphics[width=1.0\linewidth]{heatmap.png} \caption{Correlation heatmap for returns of each strategy.} \label{fig:corr}
elements.sent-2411-175: Our research has demonstrated that the application of Supervised Autoencoder de-noising in combination with Triple Barrier labeling significantly improves algorithmic trading strategies in comparison to buy and hold approaches.
elements.sent-2411-176: Our research has also addressed the following research questions:.
elements.sent-2411-177: Does data augmentation and denoising via autoencoders combined with triple barrier labeling (TBL) produce better risk-reward metrics than buy-and-hold? - As expressed by information ratio and IR*, the majority of our approaches outperforms the buy-and-hold.
elements.sent-2411-178: Does a portfolio of SAE-MLP strategies outperform a similar portfolio of buy-and-hold cryptocurrencies in terms of risk-reward metrics? - Despite the correlation of returns between strategies, combining them into a portfolio outperforms the buy-end-hold equal-weight portfolio.
elements.sent-2411-179: Despite the promising results, the study is subject to certain limitations.
elements.sent-2411-180: Firstly, the findings are based on historical data, and as such, their predictive power in relation to future performance should be considered with caution, given the volatile and evolving nature of financial markets.
elements.sent-2411-181: Secondly, the research does not take into account slippage, which could potentially diminish the net returns of the strategy, especially if dealing in illiquid markets or with large capital.
elements.sent-2411-182: The study also assumes that stop-losses and take profits from triple barrier labeling are executed immediately and perfectly, which is not always the case in the markets.
elements.sent-2411-183: This paper introduces several new approaches to algorithmic trading.
elements.sent-2411-184: First, it appears to be the first study to apply our specific model architecture in the field of algorithmic trading.
elements.sent-2411-185: This approach differs notably from the traditional models typically seen in this area.
elements.sent-2411-186: Second, while the concept of triple barrier labeling has been previously discussed, our research goes a step further by developing a specialized optimization metric designed explicitly for use with triple barrier labeling.
elements.sent-2411-187: These contributions are significant steps forward in integrating advanced machine-learning techniques into financial trading strategies.
elements.sent-2411-188: The empirical evidence indicating that algorithmic models can outperform traditional buy-and-hold strategies suggests a necessity for their adoption in asset management to enhance market efficiency and potential investment returns.
elements.sent-2411-189: Consequently, we view it as vital for regulators to craft policies that facilitate the ethical integration of these models, ensuring market fairness and stability while mitigating systemic risks.
elements.sent-2411-190: Institutional investors and fund managers are encouraged to embrace these advanced strategies, necessitating investments in technology and skilled personnel to maintain competitiveness and uphold their fiduciary responsibilities.
elements.sent-2411-191: This shift towards algorithmic trading is not only a reflection of the potential for improved financial performance but also a movement toward the inevitable modernization of financial market practices.
elements.sent-2411-192: In terms of further research, we recommend investigating other types of noise and their impacts on the strategy's performance.
elements.sent-2411-193: Additionally, the integration of slippage into the model would provide a more realistic picture of the strategy's net returns.
elements.sent-2411-194: Exploring different architectures for the autoencoder or the integration of other deep learning techniques may also yield interesting insights.
elements.sent-2411-195: The impact of these methods on other types of financial time series data, beyond the one used in this study, would also be a fruitful avenue for future research.. \bibliographystyle{ACM-Reference-Format.}
elements.sent-2411-196: Box 1212 \city{Dublin \state{Ohio \country{USA \postcode{43017-6221 rv{\"a ld rv{\"a ld Group \streetaddress{1 Th{\o rv{\"a ld Circle \city{Hekla \country{Iceland \city{Rocquencourt \country{France \streetaddress{Rono-Hills \city{Doimukh \state{Arunachal Pradesh \country{India \streetaddress{30 Shuangqing Rd \city{Haidian Qu \state{Beijing Shi \country{China \streetaddress{8600 Datapoint Drive \city{San Antonio \state{Texas \country{USA \postcode{78229 rv{\"a ld Group \streetaddress{1 Th{\o rv{\"a ld Circle \city{Hekla \country{Iceland \city{New York \country{USA \renewcommand{\shortauthors {Trovato and Tobin, et al..}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-197: A clear and well-documented \LaTeX\ document is presented as an article formatted for publication by ACM in a conference proceedings or journal publication.
elements.sent-2411-198: Based on the ``acmart'' document class, this article presents and explains many of the common variations, as well as many of the formatting elements an author may use in the preparation of the documentation of their work.
elements.sent-2411-200: ACM's consolidated article template, introduced in 2017, provides a consistent \LaTeX\ style for use across ACM publications, and incorporates accessibility and metadata-extraction functionality necessary for future Digital Library endeavors.
elements.sent-2411-201: Numerous ACM and SIG-specific \LaTeX\ templates have been examined, and their unique features incorporated into this single new template.
elements.sent-2411-202: If you are new to publishing with ACM, this document is a valuable guide to the process of preparing your work for publication.
elements.sent-2411-203: If you have published with ACM before, this document provides insight and instruction into more recent changes to the article template.
elements.sent-2411-204: The ``\verb|acmart|'' document class can be used to prepare articles for any ACM publication --- conference or journal, and for any stage of publication, from review to final ``camera-ready'' copy, to the author's own version, with { very few changes to the source.}
elements.sent-2411-205: As noted in the introduction, the ``\verb|acmart|'' document class can be used to prepare many different kinds of documentation --- a dual-anonymous initial submission of a full-length technical paper, a two-page SIGGRAPH Emerging Technologies abstract, a ``camera-ready'' journal article, a SIGCHI Extended Abstract, and more --- all by selecting the appropriate { template style and { template parameters .}}
elements.sent-2411-206: This document will explain the major features of the document class.
elements.sent-2411-207: For further information, the { \LaTeX\ User's Guide is available from \url{https://www.acm.org/publications/proceedings-template .}}
elements.sent-2411-208: The primary parameter given to the ``\verb|acmart|'' document class is the { template style which corresponds to the kind of publication or SIG publishing the work.}
elements.sent-2411-209: This parameter is enclosed in square brackets and is a part of the {\verb|documentclass| command:.}
elements.sent-2411-210: Journals use one of three template styles.
elements.sent-2411-211: All but three ACM journals use the {\verb|acmsmall| template style:.}
elements.sent-2411-212: The majority of conference proceedings documentation will use the {\verb|acmconf| template style.}
elements.sent-2411-213: In addition to specifying the { template style to be used in formatting your work, there are a number of { template parameters which modify some part of the applied template style.}}
elements.sent-2411-214: A complete list of these parameters can be found in the { \LaTeX\ User's Guide.}
elements.sent-2411-215: Frequently-used parameters, or combinations of parameters, include:.
elements.sent-2411-216: Anonymizes the work and includes line numbers.
elements.sent-2411-217: Use with the \verb|\acmSubmissionID| command to print the submission's unique ID on each page of the work.
elements.sent-2411-218: This document uses the following string as the first command in the source file:.
elements.sent-2411-219: Modifying the template --- including but not limited to: adjusting margins, typeface sizes, line spacing, paragraph and list definitions, and the use of the \verb|| command to manually adjust the vertical spacing between elements of your work --- is not allowed.
elements.sent-2411-220: The ``\verb|acmart|'' document class requires the use of the ``Libertine'' typeface family.
elements.sent-2411-221: Your \TeX\ installation should include this set of packages.
elements.sent-2411-222: Please do not substitute other typefaces.
elements.sent-2411-223: The ``\verb|lmodern|'' and ``\verb|ltimes|'' packages should not be used, as they will override the built-in typeface families.
elements.sent-2411-224: The title of your work should use capital letters appropriately - \url{https://capitalizemytitle.com/ has useful rules for capitalization.}
elements.sent-2411-225: Use the {\verb|title| command to define the title of your work.}
elements.sent-2411-226: If your work has a subtitle, define it with the {\verb|subtitle| command.}
elements.sent-2411-227: Do not insert line breaks in your title.
elements.sent-2411-228: If your title is lengthy, you must define a short version to be used in the page headers, to prevent overlapping text.
elements.sent-2411-229: The \verb|title| command has a ``short title'' parameter:.
elements.sent-2411-230: Each author must be defined separately for accurate metadata identification.
elements.sent-2411-231: Multiple authors may share one affiliation.
elements.sent-2411-232: Authors' names should not be abbreviated; use full first names wherever possible.
elements.sent-2411-233: Include authors' e-mail addresses whenever possible.
elements.sent-2411-234: Grouping authors' names or e-mail addresses, or providing an ``e-mail alias,'' as shown below, is not acceptable:.
elements.sent-2411-235: The \verb|authornote| and \verb|authornotemark| commands allow a note to apply to multiple authors --- for example, if the first two authors of an article contributed equally to the work.
elements.sent-2411-236: If your author list is lengthy, you must define a shortened version of the list of authors to be used in the page headers, to prevent overlapping text.
elements.sent-2411-237: The following command should be placed just after the last \verb|| definition:.
elements.sent-2411-238: Omitting this command will force the use of a concatenated list of all of the authors' names, which may result in overlapping text in the page headers.
elements.sent-2411-239: The article template's documentation, available at \url{https://www.acm.org/publications/proceedings-template , has a complete explanation of these commands and tips for their effective use.}
elements.sent-2411-240: Note that authors' addresses are mandatory for journal articles.
elements.sent-2411-241: Authors of any work published by ACM will need to complete a rights form.
elements.sent-2411-242: Depending on the kind of work, and the rights management choice made by the author, this may be copyright transfer, permission, license, or an OA (open access) agreement.
elements.sent-2411-243: Regardless of the rights management choice, the author will receive a copy of the completed rights form once it has been submitted.
elements.sent-2411-244: This form contains \LaTeX\ commands that must be copied into the source document.
elements.sent-2411-245: When the document source is compiled, these commands and their parameters add formatted text to several areas of the final document:.
elements.sent-2411-246: Rights information is unique to the work; if you are preparing several works for an event, make sure to use the correct set of commands with each of the works.
elements.sent-2411-247: The ACM Reference Format text is required for all articles over one page in length, and is optional for one-page articles (abstracts).
elements.sent-2411-248: Two elements of the ``acmart'' document class provide powerful taxonomic tools for you to help readers find your work in an online search.
elements.sent-2411-249: The ACM Computing Classification System --- \url{https://www.acm.org/publications/class-2012 --- is a set of classifiers and concepts that describe the computing discipline.}
elements.sent-2411-250: Authors can select entries from this classification system, via \url{https://dl.acm.org/ccs/ccs.cfm , and generate the commands to be included in the \LaTeX\ source.}
elements.sent-2411-251: User-defined keywords are a comma-separated list of words and phrases of the authors' choosing, providing a more flexible way of describing the research being presented.
elements.sent-2411-252: CCS concepts and user-defined keywords are required for for all articles over two pages in length, and are optional for one- and two-page articles (or abstracts).
elements.sent-2411-253: Your work should use standard \LaTeX\ sectioning commands: \verb|section|, \verb|subsection|, \verb|subsubsection|, and \verb|paragraph|.
elements.sent-2411-254: They should be numbered; do not remove the numbering from the commands.
elements.sent-2411-255: Simulating a sectioning command by setting the first word or words of a paragraph in boldface or italicized text is { not allowed.}
elements.sent-2411-256: The ``\verb|acmart|'' document class includes the ``\verb|booktabs|'' package --- \url{https://ctan.org/pkg/booktabs --- for preparing high-quality tables.}
elements.sent-2411-257: Table captions are placed { above the table.}
elements.sent-2411-258: Because tables cannot be split across pages, the best placement for them is typically the top of the page nearest their initial cite.
elements.sent-2411-259: To ensure this proper ``floating'' placement of tables, use the environment table to enclose the table's contents and the table caption.
elements.sent-2411-260: The contents of the table itself must go in the tabular environment, to be aligned properly in rows and columns, with the desired horizontal and vertical rules.
elements.sent-2411-261: Again, detailed instructions on tabular material are found in the \LaTeX\ User's Guide.
elements.sent-2411-262: Immediately following this sentence is the point at which Table~\ref{tab:freq is included in the input file; compare the placement of the table here with the table in the printed output of this document.}
elements.figure-2411-015: \caption{Frequency of Special Characters} \label{tab:freq} \begin{tabular}{ccl} Non-English or Math Frequency Comments \O 1 in 1,000 For Swedish names $\pi$ 1 in 5 Common in math \$ 4 in 5 Used in business $\Psi^2_1$ 1 in 40,000 Unexplained usage \end{tabular}
elements.sent-2411-263: To set a wider table, which takes up the whole width of the page's live area, use the environment table* to enclose the table's contents and the table caption.
elements.sent-2411-264: As with a single-column table, this wide table will ``float'' to a location deemed more desirable.
elements.sent-2411-265: Immediately following this sentence is the point at which Table~\ref{tab:commands is included in the input file; again, it is instructive to compare the placement of the table here with the table in the printed output of this document.}
elements.figure-2411-016: \caption{Some Typical Commands} \label{tab:commands} \begin{tabular}{ccl} Command A Number Comments \texttt{{\char'134}author} 100 Author \texttt{{\char'134}table} 300 For tables \texttt{{\char'134}table*} 400 For wider tables \end{tabular}
elements.sent-2411-266: Always use midrule to separate table header rows from data rows, and use it only for this purpose.
elements.sent-2411-267: This enables assistive technologies to recognise table headers and support their users in navigating tables more easily.
elements.sent-2411-268: You may want to display math equations in three distinct styles: inline, numbered or non-numbered display.
elements.sent-2411-269: Each of the three are discussed in the next sections.
elements.sent-2411-270: A formula that appears in the running text is called an inline or in-text formula.
elements.sent-2411-271: It is produced by the math environment, which can be invoked with the usual {\char'134begin\,\ldots{\char'134 end construction or with the short form \$\,\ldots\$.}}
elements.sent-2411-272: You can use any of the symbols and structures, from $\alpha$ to $\omega$, available in \LaTeX~\cite{Lamport:LaTeX ; this section will simply show a few examples of in-text equations in context.}
elements.sent-2411-273: Notice how this equation: \begin{math \lim_{n\rightarrow \infty x=0 \end{math , set here in in-line math style, looks slightly different when set in display style.. (See next section).}}}
elements.sent-2411-274: A numbered display equation---one set off by vertical space from the text and centered horizontally---is produced by the equation environment.
elements.sent-2411-275: An unnumbered display equation is produced by the displaymath environment.
elements.sent-2411-276: Again, in either environment, you can use any of the symbols and structures available in \LaTeX\@; this section will just give a couple of examples of display equations in context.
elements.sent-2411-277: First, consider the equation, shown as an inline equation above: \begin{equation}\lim_{n\rightarrow \infty}x=0\end{equation} Notice how it is formatted somewhat differently in the displaymath environment.
elements.sent-2411-278: Now, we'll enter an unnumbered equation: \begin{displaymath \sum_{i=0 ^{\infty x + 1 \end{displaymath and follow it with another numbered equation: \begin{equation}\sum_{i=0}^{\infty}x_i=\int_{0}^{\pi+2} f\end{equation} just to demonstrate \LaTeX's able handling of numbering.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-279: The ``\verb|figure|'' environment should be used for figures.
elements.sent-2411-280: One or more images can be placed within a figure.
elements.sent-2411-281: If your figure contains third-party material, you must clearly identify it as such, as shown in the example below.
elements.figure-2411-017: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{sample-franklin} \caption{1907 Franklin Model D roadster. Photograph by Harris \& Ewing, Inc. [Public domain], via Wikimedia Commons. (\url{https://goo.gl/VLCRBB}).} \Description{A woman and a girl in white dresses sit in an open car.}
elements.sent-2411-282: Your figures should contain a caption which describes the figure to the reader.
elements.sent-2411-283: Figure captions are placed { below the figure.}
elements.sent-2411-284: Every figure should also have a figure description unless it is purely decorative.
elements.sent-2411-285: These descriptions convey what’s in the image to someone who cannot see it.
elements.sent-2411-286: They are also used by search engine crawlers for indexing images, and when images cannot be loaded.
elements.sent-2411-287: A figure description must be unformatted plain text less than 2000 characters long (including spaces).
elements.sent-2411-288: For figures that convey important and complex new information, a short text description may not be adequate.
elements.sent-2411-289: More complex alternative descriptions can be placed in an appendix and referenced in a short figure description.
elements.sent-2411-290: For example, provide a data table capturing the information in a bar chart, or a structured list representing a graph.
elements.sent-2411-291: For additional information regarding how best to write figure descriptions and why doing this is so important, please see \url{https://www.acm.org/publications/taps/describing-figures/ .}
elements.sent-2411-292: A ``teaser figure'' is an image, or set of images in one figure, that are placed after all author and affiliation information, and before the body of the article, spanning the page.
elements.sent-2411-293: If you wish to have such a figure in your article, place the command immediately before the \verb|| command:.
elements.sent-2411-294: The use of \BibTeX\ for the preparation and formatting of one's references is strongly recommended.
elements.sent-2411-295: Authors' names should be complete --- use full first names (``Donald E.
elements.sent-2411-297: Knuth'') --- and the salient identifying features of a reference should be included: title, year, volume, number, pages, article DOI, etc. The bibliography is included in your source document with these two commands, placed just before the \verb|.
